title: Everyone Is Brave Now
body:
I have been thinking about courage, which is a thing I would not ordinarily recommend. Courage, in my experience, is better practiced than contemplated. But it has become so conspicuous lately, so exhibited and branded and rewarded with engagement metrics, that one can hardly think of anything else.

Everyone is brave now. This is the remarkable fact of 2026. Not merely the soldiers in whatever theater of conflict we are currently not paying sufficient attention to, not merely the people making the actual difficult choices that the world requires — but everyone. The executive who tells his conference audience about the time he cried in a Hertz rental car. The wellness founder who reveals, at considerable length, the childhood wound that led her to start a company selling supplements. The celebrity who posts an unfiltered photograph and the one who posts a post about how brave the first celebrity was for posting an unfiltered photograph. We are all, every last one of us, incredibly brave.

I find I have some thoughts.

In my time, which was not a gentle time by any measure, we were suspicious of public emotion. This was not because we lacked feeling — I assure you, we did not. I wrote enough verse about heartbreak to fill several small volumes and damage several reputations, including my own. But we understood, in a way that seems to have been misplaced somewhere between the talk-show couch and the smartphone camera, that feelings displayed too loudly in public were feelings that had been prepared for an audience. And feelings prepared for an audience are performances, not confessions. A confession happens between you and a priest, or you and your mirror. What happens between you and two million followers is something else entirely, and it has a different price.

The price, in my era, was your dignity. The bargain you made when you chose discretion was this: you got to keep the secret shape of your private suffering, and in exchange you were not permitted to use it as leverage. You could not say *I am brave* because you had told people about your difficult quarter. You could not sell your breakdown as a brand differentiator. You simply had to carry the thing, which was heavy, and get on with it, which was also heavy, and if you were very good at both, occasionally someone might notice and say nothing at all, which was its own kind of reward.

This arrangement has been discontinued.

What we have now is the *therapeutic marketplace*, in which vulnerability is the principal currency and the returns are, I am told, exceptional. The algorithm, that vast and impersonal machine which replaced the editor and the Round Table and every other mechanism we once had for determining who deserved an audience, has decided that raw emotion drives engagement. And since raw emotion drives engagement, raw emotion is what we produce. It is produced at volume, at pace, with production values that would have seemed extraordinary for a major motion picture fifteen years ago. The CEO's panic attack is shot in warm natural light. The influencer's grief is color-graded. The breakdown is bookended by advertisements for the very supplements the breakdown presumably made necessary.

I want to be careful here. I do not doubt that some of these people are suffering. I have suffered considerably myself, and I would not wish to trivialize the experience for anyone. What I am questioning is not the suffering but the *framing*. And the framing, in every case, is the same: I was broken, I found my way through, I am here to tell you about it, and also to sell you something — which may be a course or a candle or simply the ongoing reassurance that I exist and am brave.

The thing about genuine courage — and I speak as someone who was arrested for the crime of standing on a sidewalk in Boston to protest an execution, which has never quite made it onto anyone's inspiration reel — is that it does not know it is being courageous. It is too busy being afraid and continuing anyway. The woman I met in Madrid in 1937, making soup over a stove while the city was shelled, was not considering her personal brand. She was making soup. The making of the soup was the whole of it.

What our current bravery industry sells us is precisely the opposite: the story of the making of the soup, told in retrospect, with a redemptive arc, a call to action, and usually a discount code. The soup itself has grown cold. The soup does not matter. What matters is that you *had feelings about* the soup, and that those feelings were, in the end, *growth*.

I am not against growth. I am against growth as a product.

The particular genius of this arrangement is that it has found a way to monetize the most private experiences while simultaneously insulating itself from criticism. To suggest that a person's public vulnerability is a performance is to be *unkind*, which is now the worst thing you can be. To observe that the disclosure of trauma for profit might be doing something complicated — not to the audience, who are largely grown adults capable of managing their own scrolling, but to the disclosed trauma itself, which is being extracted and processed and packaged in ways that may or may not leave anything behind — this is to be *toxic*, which is the word we have chosen to mean "says a thing I'd rather not examine."

In my years as a drama critic, I was occasionally accused of cruelty. I was not cruel. I was exact. Cruelty enjoys the wound. Exactness is simply unwilling to pretend the wound is something other than a wound. The distinction matters enormously, and in the current climate it has been completely abolished. To call something by its right name is now cruelty. To call something by a wrong name, as long as the wrong name is kinder, is now kindness. I do not know what we gain from this arrangement, but I know what we lose: the ability to say what things actually are.

What the public breakdown actually is, in most cases, is a transaction. And there is nothing wrong with transactions — I have made several, in publishing and in Hollywood and in life, and I have found them to be more honest than most of what passes for honesty. The difficulty is the insistence that the transaction is something else. The person performing their vulnerability for an audience does not say *I am making a transaction*. They say *I am being brave*. And then we are all required to agree, and to say that they are being brave, and the agreement itself becomes the product.

I have spent considerable portions of my life in rooms where people were performing virtue. The drawing rooms of New York, the parlors of Hollywood, the committees of various well-intentioned organizations in which the stated purpose was justice and the actual purpose was the feeling of being a person who cares about justice. I have seen the performance of liberalism by people who would cross the street to avoid the actual liberal cause that was inconveniencing them. I know the particular quality of the room when everyone is performing for everyone else and no one is saying what they are actually thinking.

That quality has not changed. Only the room has gotten larger, and the performance has better lighting, and what was once contained to a particular class of people with access to drawing rooms has been democratized entirely, so that anyone with a phone and a difficult childhood can now participate.

The difficult childhood, by the way, has never been more in demand. I had one myself, and I can tell you that in my time it was not considered an *asset*. It was considered a wound, which you carried, and did not exhibit, and occasionally wrote about in a way that told the truth without quite telling it. This was called *craft*. Craft is out of fashion. Disclosure is in. Disclosure without craft, without the pressure that craft applies to experience, is simply the experience itself — hot, formless, and convinced of its own importance.

I find I am, after all this, still not against courage. I am for it, in fact, quite passionately. I think the world at this particular juncture has urgent need of it. I would simply like to suggest that real courage tends to be quieter than this, tends to cost more than this, and tends not to come with a comment section.

The bravest thing I ever did, I have never told anyone about. That is how I know it was brave.
